topicArea: Sociology of Time, Clock Discipline & the Question of Who Sets the Tempo

questions.passage:
Before the factory bell, work in most places answered to the task rather than to the hour. Grain was cut when ripe and for as long as light and weather held; nets were mended between tides; the rhythm of labor bunched and slackened with the season, the church calendar, and the job itself, and the notion of selling a stretch of abstract time, empty hours, detached from any particular task, would have struck most working people as close to unintelligible. The transition to industrial employment was therefore not merely a change of workplace but a change in the texture of time, and contemporaries experienced it as such: the earliest generations of factory workers fought the clock, arriving by the sun, absenting themselves for feast days the calendar no longer recognized, and treating the timekeeper's ledger as an instrument of fraud, which, given the frequency with which factory clocks were set to favor the master, it often enough was.

The clock's partisans had a case that went beyond discipline for its own sake. Synchronization is a genuine productive force. A power loom idle because its minder is absent idles the capital of the whole shed; a railway cannot run on task time, and the standardization of hours across regions, one of the more consequential and least celebrated reforms of the industrial age, made possible coordination at scales no tide or harvest could ever have organized. Whatever else the regime of hours accomplished, it solved a real problem that the older temporal order could not.

What followed the workers' initial resistance is, for the sociologist, the more interesting phase: the discipline moved inside. Within a few generations, punctuality had become a virtue taught in schools and sermons, lateness a moral failing rather than a scheduling variance, and the vocabulary of accountancy had colonized time itself, time spent, saved, wasted, budgeted, invested, as though the hours were coin. Workers who had once struck against the clock came to strike by it, demanding the ten-hour day, then the eight, conducting the struggle over time entirely within the clock's own categories. The measure that had been imposed as an instrument of control was now the shared and largely unquestioned medium in which control was negotiated.

It is tempting to narrate all this as a fall, from an organic, humane temporality into a mechanical one, and a whole literature of nostalgia has done so. The temptation should be resisted on the evidence. Task time was no idyll: its rhythms bound workers to daylight and season with a rigidity no statute softened; its slack periods were often enforced idleness shadowed by scarcity rather than leisure; and its allocation within the household was steeply unequal, with the flexibility so admired in retrospect largely enjoyed by those whose claims on others' labor made their own time elastic. Whatever the clock imposed, it also created the very possibility of the bounded working day, and thus of time that was contractually, defensibly, one's own, a possibility task time, which recognized no boundary between work and life because it never needed one, could not supply.

The deeper change, then, is best described neither as speed nor as mechanization but as a change in ownership. When time became measurable in uniform units, it became alienable, something that could be cut into lengths, priced, and transferred, and the wage bargain became, at bottom, a sale of hours. From that construction flow both faces of the modern temporal order: the employer's entitlement to fill the purchased hours as densely as possible, and the worker's counter-entitlement to the unpurchased remainder. The clock did not merely discipline time; it converted time into property, and property, unlike rhythm, can be contested, regulated, and redistributed.

The present has complicated the picture without resolving it. Flexible scheduling, remote work, and piece-rate platforms have dissolved the factory's sharp boundary and returned something task-like to labor's rhythm, and this is routinely described, by those who design such arrangements, as a restoration of autonomy. The description deserves suspicion. Where the older task orientation was governed by the work itself, the newer flexibility is frequently governed by continuous demand, monitored remotely and priced by the assignment, so that the bounded day dissolves not into self-direction but into availability without limit. Flexibility, it turns out, names two opposite conditions, control over one's own tempo and exposure to everyone else's, and which of the two a worker enjoys is determined less by the schedule's shape than by the power behind it.

The clock, in other words, was never the protagonist. Temporal regimes distribute advantage, and the same instrument that regimented the mill also anchored the eight-hour day; the same flexibility that frees the consultant strands the courier. The recurring question is not whether life should run faster or slower, nor whether measured time is authentic, but the one the first factory hands grasped immediately and their descendants keep having to relearn: who sets the tempo, and who must answer to it.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The shift from task time to clock time is best understood not as mechanization or moral decline but as a transformation in who owns and controls time, a question that persists in altered form within contemporary flexible work arrangements.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pre-industrial task time offered workers an organic and humane temporality that industrial clock discipline destroyed and that modern flexibility is now restoring.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The standardization of hours was the most consequential reform of the industrial age, since it enabled railway coordination across regions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Workers' internalization of punctuality proves that any imposed discipline eventually becomes a genuine virtue.

explanations.A: Correct. The passage traces the transition from task time to clock time, resists the nostalgic fall-narrative with evidence of task time's rigidities and inequalities, identifies the deeper change as time becoming alienable property whose ownership can be contested, and shows that contemporary flexibility re-poses rather than resolves the question, concluding that what matters is who sets the tempo and who must answer to it.
explanations.B: This choice combines two claims the passage explicitly argues against. The fourth paragraph dismantles the idyll of task time, bound to daylight, shadowed by scarcity, unequally distributed within households, and the sixth paragraph treats the description of modern flexibility as restored autonomy with explicit suspicion, since it often delivers availability without limit.
explanations.C: This choice inflates a supporting observation into the thesis. The second paragraph does credit standardized hours as consequential and under-celebrated, but the passage's argument runs through internalized discipline, the conversion of time into property, and contemporary flexibility, none of which this choice touches.
explanations.D: This choice draws a general moral lesson the passage never asserts. The third paragraph describes punctuality's transformation into a taught virtue as a sociological development, the discipline moving inside, without endorsing the internalized norm as genuine virtue or generalizing to all imposed disciplines.

questions.stem: The primary function of the fourth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: concede that the earliest factory workers were wrong to resist the introduction of clock discipline.

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove that leisure was unknown before the industrial era.

choices.label: C
choices.text: resist the nostalgic reading of the transition as a fall from humane temporality, by cataloguing task time's own rigidities and inequities and crediting the clock with making the bounded working day possible.

choices.label: D
choices.text: introduce the concept of time as alienable property that the fifth paragraph develops.

explanations.A: This choice confuses correcting a later nostalgia with retroactively faulting the original resisters. The first paragraph presents the workers' resistance sympathetically, noting that factory clocks were often set fraudulently; the fourth paragraph's target is the literature of nostalgia, not the workers themselves.
explanations.B: This choice overstates one evidentiary detail into an absolute claim. The paragraph says task time's slack periods were often enforced idleness shadowed by scarcity rather than leisure, a corrective about how slack time functioned, not a denial that leisure existed anywhere before industrialization.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph opens by naming the temptation to narrate the transition as a fall, states that the temptation should be resisted on the evidence, and supplies that evidence: task time's rigidity under daylight and season, its scarcity-shadowed idleness, its unequal household allocation, and finally the clock's creation of the bounded, contractually defensible day that task time could not supply.
explanations.D: This choice assigns the paragraph the following paragraph's work. The conversion of time into alienable, priced, transferable property is introduced and developed in the fifth paragraph; the fourth is occupied with dismantling the nostalgic narrative.

questions.stem: The observation that workers who had once struck against the clock came to strike by it, in the third paragraph, is offered as evidence that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: labor organizing became less effective once workers accepted industrial employment.

choices.label: B
choices.text: the clock's categories had become the shared, largely unquestioned medium within which even resistance to employers was conducted.

choices.label: C
choices.text: employers eventually abandoned the use of timekeeping to control their workforces.

choices.label: D
choices.text: the eight-hour day was won primarily through appeals to pre-industrial task rhythms.

explanations.A: This choice reads a claim about the form of struggle as a claim about its effectiveness. The paragraph notes that workers demanded and pursued the ten-hour and then the eight-hour day, campaigns the passage treats as consequential, so diminished effectiveness is neither stated nor implied.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph's point is that discipline moved inside: the measure imposed as an instrument of control became the medium in which control was negotiated, so that even the struggle over time was conducted entirely within the clock's own categories, hours demanded, counted, and defended, rather than by rejecting measured time itself.
explanations.C: This choice contradicts the passage's whole trajectory. The wage bargain as a sale of hours, developed in the fifth paragraph, presupposes continued timekeeping, and remote monitoring in the sixth paragraph shows temporal control persisting in new forms rather than being abandoned.
explanations.D: This choice supplies a basis for the eight-hour campaign that the paragraph rules out. Striking by the clock means the demand was framed in the clock's units, bounded hours, not in appeals to the task rhythms the older order had followed.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the claim that contemporary flexible work arrangements restore workers' autonomy is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: accepting, since flexibility returns labor to the task orientation the passage praises as humane.

choices.label: B
choices.text: dismissive of flexibility in all its forms, since the passage argues that bounded factory hours should be universally restored.

choices.label: C
choices.text: neutral, since the passage regards schedule design as a technical question outside its concerns.

choices.label: D
choices.text: suspicious, since flexibility governed by continuous monitored demand can dissolve the bounded day into unlimited availability, making the schedule's meaning depend on the power behind it.

explanations.A: This choice errs twice: the passage never praises task orientation as humane, having spent the fourth paragraph on its rigidities, and the sixth paragraph explicitly says the autonomy description deserves suspicion because the new flexibility is often governed by continuous demand rather than by the work itself.
explanations.B: This choice converts targeted suspicion into blanket condemnation and adds a prescription the passage never issues. The sixth paragraph acknowledges that flexibility can mean control over one's own tempo, the consultant's case, so flexibility as such is not dismissed, and no restoration of factory hours is proposed.
explanations.C: This choice mistakes the passage's central concern for a side issue. The distribution of temporal power, who sets the tempo, is the essay's announced through-line, and the sixth paragraph engages schedule design precisely because it determines whether flexibility means self-direction or exposure.
explanations.D: Correct. The sixth paragraph states that the restoration-of-autonomy description deserves suspicion, distinguishes the older task orientation governed by the work from newer flexibility governed by continuous, remotely monitored demand, and concludes that flexibility names two opposite conditions, with the difference determined by the power behind the schedule rather than its shape.

questions.stem: A delivery platform advertises that its couriers enjoy complete freedom, since they choose when to log on and accept assignments. Couriers report that pay rates fluctuate minute to minute with demand, that declining assignments lowers their priority for future offers, and that earning a living requires remaining logged on and responsive through most waking hours. Based on the passage, this arrangement is best characterized as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a restoration of pre-industrial task orientation, since the couriers' work rhythm is governed by the tasks themselves.

choices.label: B
choices.text: an example of the bounded working day the fifth paragraph credits the clock with creating.

choices.label: C
choices.text: flexibility as exposure to others' tempo rather than control over one's own, since the schedule's nominal openness is governed by continuous priced demand and effective penalties for unavailability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: evidence that temporal discipline has been abolished by platform technology.

explanations.A: This choice accepts the platform's framing while missing the passage's distinction. The older task orientation was governed by the work itself, the harvest, the tide, whereas the couriers' rhythm is governed by fluctuating priced demand and priority penalties, which the sixth paragraph identifies as precisely what makes the new flexibility unlike the old.
explanations.B: This choice inverts the scenario's structure. The bounded day is defined by a defensible limit separating purchased hours from one's own; couriers who must remain logged on through most waking hours to earn a living have lost exactly that boundary, experiencing its dissolution rather than its protection.
explanations.C: Correct. The arrangement matches the sixth paragraph's suspicious case point for point: nominal freedom over when to work, combined with minute-to-minute pricing, penalties for declining, and the practical necessity of constant availability, delivers exposure to everyone else's tempo rather than control over one's own, with the difference determined by the power behind the schedule.
explanations.D: This choice mistakes the transformation of discipline for its abolition. The passage's final paragraphs argue that monitoring and demand-pricing continue temporal control in a new form; a system that penalizes unavailability is exercising discipline, not dispensing with it.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the fifth paragraph's claim that the conversion of time into property made temporal arrangements contestable and regulable in ways the older rhythmic order could not support?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that mechanical clocks became affordable to ordinary households earlier than historians previously believed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Comparative historical evidence that legal limits on the working day, overtime compensation, and enforceable rest entitlements emerged only in economies where labor was bought in standardized time units, and proved unformulable in systems where work obligations were defined by task and season, because those systems offered no unit in which a limit could be stated or enforced.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that early factory owners frequently manipulated workplace clocks to lengthen the working day.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that most modern workers underestimate the number of hours they work each week.

explanations.A: This choice dates the diffusion of an instrument without addressing the claim about contestability. Household clock ownership says nothing about whether measured time enabled forms of legal limitation and redistribution that rhythmic time could not support.
explanations.B: Correct. The claim is that property-like time, cut into uniform units, priced, and transferred, can be contested, regulated, and redistributed as rhythm cannot. Evidence that day limits, overtime, and rest entitlements arose only where labor was sold in standard units, and could not even be formulated where obligations were task-defined for want of a unit to state the limit in, directly ties the regulability to the unit structure the paragraph identifies.
explanations.C: This choice supports the first paragraph's remark about fraudulent factory clocks but bears on the abuse of measurement, not on measurement's role in enabling contestation and regulation, which is the fifth paragraph's distinct claim.
explanations.D: This choice concerns the accuracy of workers' self-reports, a measurement-error question detached from the historical claim about what forms of regulation the unit structure of time made possible.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the passage's claim that the vocabulary of accountancy colonized time only after, and as a consequence of, industrial clock discipline?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Extensive documentary evidence that agricultural and artisanal communities organized entirely by task and season nonetheless routinely spoke of time as spent, saved, wasted, and owed, in commercial metaphors predating factory employment by centuries.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that modern workers use accounting metaphors for time more frequently in the workplace than at home.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Evidence that some industrial-era schools taught punctuality more zealously than others.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that the eight-hour-day campaigns borrowed organizational tactics from earlier religious movements.

explanations.A: Correct. The third paragraph presents the accountancy vocabulary, time spent, saved, wasted, budgeted, as part of the internalization that followed industrial discipline. Documentary evidence that fully task-and-season communities used the same commercial metaphors centuries before factories would show the vocabulary preceding the discipline credited with producing it, directly undermining the claimed sequence and causal relationship.
explanations.B: This choice compares contexts of modern usage without addressing historical origin. Whether accounting metaphors cluster at work or at home today says nothing about whether they arose before or after industrial clock discipline.
explanations.C: This choice documents variation in the intensity of punctuality instruction, which is fully compatible with the passage's account of internalization. Uneven zeal among schools does not touch the sequence in which the accountancy vocabulary emerged.
explanations.D: This choice traces the tactics of labor campaigns to earlier movements, a matter of organizational lineage unrelated to when commercial metaphors for time entered common speech.
